speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that there were gay prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, in the small room everybody knows about everybody. And there was also joking about two others. And due to this reason I recognize it then. And then I went to them-- to Yanush-- and asked him, from where are you? He told me from Torun. I never seen him, because I-- I was never going in such special places. And the other one also was from Torun. And only in this way we have met us there. But with Yanush-- before, I have to mention, because from this Koronowo prison, every week we're sent to the transportation to Zuzenhausen and to Dachau. Everybody was very anxious of this transportation. And it once happened that Yanush was sent before I was wondering when I had seen-- I met him in Torun after the war and that he was alive. Maybe only then because he was 27 or 26 years old. He was rather strong. And he [INAUDIBLE] at this life. We didn't want to talk about-- he talk-- only once time he said, you are lucky that you were not there. You're lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the time that all the three of you were in Koronowo, did that help you? Did that support you? Or where did you find friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh you mean the-- [INAUDIBLE] no, I was always-- this was so bad situation. I was hungry. I-- I-- I was-- I was missing my family. And even if met to-- on the contrary, it, for me, was better not to-- because there were too many jokes. And then you cheer up. You go away. Even when we went-- once per week we have to go to the douche. You know, it was-- no douche. It was maybe cold water douche. Because it was cold and in a very quick way.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then some would joke, be careful. Maybe some-- because it was so stupid. They joked about we will do something, you know? Even in this place.

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, everybody was slim, bone, skin only, never had-- thinking about-- especially me. I had no experience even that-- what I did with Willi it was like play-- let's say play. Of course, it should be lie that my sleeves on my-- my trousers were not wet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even he-- but I had to do so home before I gave my mother for washing that there would be no traces of something. You understand what I mean? Yeah? But in the prison? No. Never. I had never no erection. I knew later-- but never during all that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: It means that no food, no vitamins, that the body was weak. And then I-- you know, probably you heard about my leg-- my right leg. It was swollen. It was ill. It was a big problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it only joking from other prisoners? Or, were you're also treated worse in Koronowo?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, treated-- beaten, we cannot say. No, it wasn't-- but it was enough. It was enough. It was a rather not good. Later, when I succeed to receive this better job, they had some respect of me. Because you see, nobody could do it. As I told you once the [NON-ENGLISH] came to our cell and wanted to-- one-- the called [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] it means writer. And I stood up. I said. And he took me for the examination. I never forget it. It was exactly in German, [SPEAKING GERMAN] That was all. And you see, he took this paper. He said, my [NON-ENGLISH] was rather good. And, oh, good. OK. You'll be the writer. Go to-- run to the cell. Took you-- what I have to take. Maybe only the, you know, the-- the-- the metallic plate for this my spoon, and something else, and that was all. And I went to the three man room. One was working in the camera room, the other one in the kitchen, and me as a writer. My job was, I have to go to the [NON-ENGLISH] room to make all the the book-- the book keeping of the name of prisoners everyday. It was rather a good job. I could-- I was not so close. I belonged to the high society, as we called, in the-- in the prison. Therefore, if I visited my old cell with the 40 prisoners, even with the two Polish guys-- but one was sent to the-- I didn't see it, only the other one. The Apfelbaum was still there. And all of them-- most-- not [INAUDIBLE] but the Apfelbaum, they congratulated me for this job. They were very glad that I succeeded. And especially Apfelbaum, you see, boy, you are young. I told you, you will be alive. But don't forget about us. I never forget. And I also not forget once the-- I came with some bread. I have cigarettes, also. Because, you see, even in the prison it's like small city. They make the exchange. I never smoked cigarettes. But for cigarettes, like money this was. I could buy a little margarine, some bread, some potato, boiling potato. And I took with me some of this food. And I went to the cell. I wanted to give not only to Apfelbaum, but also to some other people, you know, this gay from Torun, this [INAUDIBLE]. And there was also-- I had some-- I don't know the name-- [INAUDIBLE]. Maybe also-- but no gay, he was hetero.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I asked, where is Apfelbaum? They told me, no. He was a few days ago very ill. He had every day this diarrhea. He couldn't use of it. He made in the bed. And the mafia, what is this smell? We want still alive. And then maybe they beat him. I don't know. Anyhow, somebody told me that with some glasses he made self-- [NON-ENGLISH]. So that was also very, very sad information for me. And I-- I lost a very-- especially at the beginning when he gave me the trust for the life, for everything. It was very important during the very heavy time. Where did he come form?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think from Lodz. He told me from Lodz. You see, some places, some dates, some things I know very exactly. But there are some, I don't know what happened. I cannot remember. Even when I am saying later, later. When I was-- I was sent to the Germany prison, I remember the way. I remember there was-- first we walked in snow. Then they took by-- I have seen in Holocaust Museums this rail car in which we are sent like for animals. But then it was silent. And I cannot remember how we were sent to the-- it should be a [INAUDIBLE]-- but I don't know. Maybe I was-- I don't know. Maybe it was during the night that I haven't seen. Or, maybe-- oh, it could be maybe it was dark, you see, before we were sent. This part is for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you know-- do you know anything more about Apfelbaum? His profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. No. Only-- he-- what I know, he spoke very good German, much better than me. And sometimes-- because, you must know, we, in the-- in the prison, we had to write-- we had the permission once every three months to write a letter to family, but only in German language. It should be-- it had be [NON-ENGLISH] control-- control. And usually, before I came, he wrote. But then I helped him. Sometimes I needed some words in German. And he helped me how it would be German, German. And so-- and he said to me, do that. Because the prison, they give you one potato for this writing. I am ashamed, even now, that I have taken from this friend. But he said, no, one potato is not enough, more. You did more for them than was one potato, because they are very glad if they receive some news from the family. And most of them, they were also in the prison. Only the four, but they black market. They made-- let's see vodka, schnapps. There were also some witness of Jehovah. Gypsy was also. There were green-- I don't know exactly. [NON-ENGLISH] This is Green-- some sector of religion. Also, in the prison there are very poor people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only the four that believe in one God, they came here. Just now, after my long, long time alive. [INAUDIBLE] thinking of what did Hitler did with the people? He wanted only pure ration of people. And you see here in America, it's international. So many mixed people, and they are so nice in one source to make the people, because they said-- they said, I have to state that I am not-- [INAUDIBLE] Irish? What is Irish? I didn't know what's Irish. Irish it means I am not Jewish? Probably yeah, or not? He wanted to-- that all people is pure German. Did you recognize people when you say there were gypsies and there were Jehovah's Witnesses? Did they wear markings or triangles, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but, you know, when I wrote the letter, then my friendship-- I wanted to know just a little-- who is this? If, maybe, there should be murder from the clan. I refused to-- I refused to [INAUDIBLE]. I don't want your potato. I don't write. But when he came and this and said-- and then I-- I-- I had to translate-- not only to write, but also translate. Because even the letter from there had to be also in the German. And I translated them.

speaker: interviewee
text: You understand? Then I knew the reason and my contact to them to these people was completely different than to the crimes. There were some crimes for murder, especially the number the boss, one. You said earlier that Apfelbaum did wear a star of David. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he had the David-- over here. On here he had. He had the David. Because we had here-- I don't know also-- we had blue or dark dresses with some stripe. But there was no so different color like in the-- I knew in the country was pink color. No, he was not. Because few of homosexuals in concentration with more-- and he in prison in my cell only with me to get a three.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course then, as I was writer, I met other. As a doctor was work also one Polish gay. What kind of position? Oh, there was also another one. But I don't know-- forget the name.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was treated very good. He was-- he was working together with the office, with the [NON-ENGLISH]. It was very high position.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they gypsies and the Jehovah's Witnesses didn't wear any special marking?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. Even we or the other-- no. In my cell maybe only three- there were two Jews, one younger and one-- only two. The other one, I don't know. Because my contact was most to Apfelbaum.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Koronowo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there more than one year. Yeah, one year, especially when I had this nice position. And once they came also to me, you must go. You will not-- oh, the first thing I was thinking maybe to Zuzenhausen. I didn't ask. And I think I knew what I was [INAUDIBLE]. No, first I have to take all my civil dressing. It was in some spacial basket. Not basket-- sack. And then he gave me the [NON-ENGLISH]. I-- I was sent to this-- to-- I will go with him. But first, later, I heard I'm going to Stuhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought, maybe is this-- I never heard about Stuhm. I thought, maybe is this concentration camp? He said, don't be worried. You will be very good. Your leg is so ill. Don't worry. They will be very good. It will be better treated.

speaker: interviewee
text: And yes, when we came-- when we arrived at Stuhm, I was sent immediately to a lazarette-- a lazarette, or clinic, for prisoners. There was some-- I remember there were some Ukrainian boy, from Holland, Belgium, Norway. And that's all maybe. And maybe-- oh, and one Polish from [INAUDIBLE] near of Torun. And I was there maybe three months. It was the best time, of course, without working. The food was a little better. They tried to-- to make me healthy with this. But they couldn't fix it. Because they had no-- in the prison-- medicine. They had to give to the front, to soldiers, but not to the prison. And after three months, I'll never forget it, came-- we called him [NON-ENGLISH]. Because this [NON-ENGLISH] was very bad and very ugly. He looked like a horse. And we called him [NON-ENGLISH]. He said, oh, you [NON-ENGLISH]. You would like to hear, he is no pencil. He is not janitorial. You must go now, work. You must all do something for us, for Germany. And I knew, because I heard that even in Warsaw was [NON-ENGLISH], revolution. Then I saw that maybe the front is coming near. Because he said, you will go, and you will [NON-ENGLISH]. Oh, I never forget this word, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: How it will be English? Do you know how? Let's say, OK, [NON-ENGLISH]. And so I was sent to the one cell-- one cell room. And every morning after so-called breakfast, I went to the [NON-ENGLISH] about hundred or two hundred prisoners. And we had to [INAUDIBLE] a special route for the soldiers, for the front of soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: I met also there the Ukraine boy. I didn't meet this Polish boy. I don't know, maybe he was there. Because even in this lazarette, also, the diarrhea. And once I have seen from Norway, what it was very, very bad condition. When he couldn't stop, he made in the-- in the bed. They took him. Probably they give him injection for the-- for the death, for the--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of prison was Stuhm?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was for, so-called, young prisoners-- for young prisoners. And later-- later I heard that it-- I don't know even just now-- I don't know who told me that my mother probably, she was suffering very much. She-- she wanted to do everything. And probably she signed a special agreement that she agreed to be German citizen. And they treated me later a little better. And they sent me from this very bad prison to Stuhm. Stuhm was completely better than-- than Koronowo. I never seen the Jews there. Only-- but there were some international-- but only young-- young, maximum 25. There was 23 that was maximum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where there any prisoners who were gay in Stuhm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes. I met one from Gdansk. [? There was ?] also, tall one. He was the [INAUDIBLE], but the name I don't know. Then I was one player-- artist, also. But he was German, I think so. He was German, yeah. Because he went-- he was later-- when I was sent to Graudenz, to Graudenz, this player was sent also to Graudenz. And I met him, also. And some of the prisoners in Graudenz, when they joke, they wanted that he sing something. And he sang some for them. And then they gave him some-- a little bread or this, like this. They-- because evening, after the work, they want to be fun, maybe or some-- to see some-- some-- some show.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he sing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, what did he sing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you see I cannot remember. But I-- [NON-ENGLISH], German [NON-ENGLISH], German hit, German hit. Once, maybe I will remember. Because once was very [INAUDIBLE]. But at this moment I cannot remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: If we go back to Stuhm for a moment--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned two-- also two young gay people. Where there other people you remember who were gay as well in Stuhm?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, my contact was very limited. I was staying in a one room cell. Once things happened-- especially in Stuhm, we had-- everybody in prison had on the door a card for what kind of reason he's here. And on my card was written, 'Unzucht mit tieren.' Unzucht mit tieren? I had never touched dog or cat. And I was so angry. And I tried two times the correction of this card. I said-- because I have to ask [NON-ENGLISH] for the permission to-- please, I had never with animal something. And he has written that I have it with animals. And he said, [SPEAKING GERMAN] He told in this way. But they changed. There was [INAUDIBLE], only unzucht.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it say fornication with men? Or, did it say just fornication when they changed it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, I don't know. One day, I see when I returned from [NON-ENGLISH]. I have seen with pen was [INAUDIBLE] there was only unzucht. Who did that? I don't know. Maybe the [INAUDIBLE] has permission, the order, to do this. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in Stuhm? Do you have a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't know. You know, I was so isolated, this-- I know only about 200 prisons were going always to this commander, [NON-ENGLISH] commander every day. It's happened maybe-- I work-- this-- this was hard, rather hard work. But the-- the-- the dinner, or lunch, was a little better. The soup-- we called-- when we put the leoffel, the spoon, it was in the soap so standing, it means it's good. But there was no fat, no meat, only maybe some-- maybe some-- I don't know. But anyhow, it was a little better than the soup like in-- in Koronowo. And I was not so hungry. Especially when I went to [INAUDIBLE] I found that some [INAUDIBLE]. I remember this from Ukraine. He come to me. We cut it. We eat the straw. I wanted to fulfill the stomach. If the stomach is filled, it's not necessary with what, you don't feel the hungry. But if the stomach is empty, you feel always hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you transferred to after Stuhm?

speaker: interviewee
text: After Stuhm, they sent whole [NON-ENGLISH] to Graudenz, the all. And there I have never heard about this prison, only barracks. Barracks like-- exactly like in concentration camp with the-- around the barracks with the wires. And they're [INAUDIBLE] always. If somebody would like escape, there is electrician. On each corner, there was a tower, this. But the barracks-- oh, there were-- with some heatings-- we heated-- we received some wood to heat. And we were also so 25 or 30 prison in each. In-- in-- this prison-- in the Graudenz, I had very good contact to one Dutch boy. His was Hendrik. The second name I don't know. But Hendrik I never forget, because he was very kind to me. He knew that I'm very weak and very sensible. So that when it was-- it was my duty to take this-- you know, we had no toilet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I have seen in American movie, there is-- there is water-- running water. There is toilet. They have TV even. But nothing. We had only this small-- this for heating and a special two-- it depends how many-- two many [INAUDIBLE] for this toilet-- for using this. And every second [? or four ?] day, you have the duty to [INAUDIBLE] to go and to change a little to clean with something against the bacterias or like this. And it was for me very heavy, because it was so full, you know. It was so-- [INAUDIBLE] piss and shit. And this Hendrik, I-- I do this for you. You can't do this. He did for me this. Oh, why? You are so weak. And then our contact was so good, because he was living there. I was living down. He joked, but no-- but, of course, they knew. Stefan, you told us about Graudenz. When you came there, there were only barracks. How many barracks were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so about 10, let's say. Because we-- not for sleeping. They're also for-- for kitchen, for magazine, for some offices. Or-- or, for-- in some barracks, they were living also the [NON-ENGLISH]. They were living the same place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it part of a prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, I was never. And before the war, I never head about this. Maybe the barracks, probably they built special. Maybe the Stuhm was overcrowded, and they built it. I don't know exactly. Probably in the [INAUDIBLE], they should know the reason. Because they said that this belong to the [INAUDIBLE]. Somebody told me that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was-- who were the guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the room, you mean? In the-- oh, there was nice fellow. I-- I tell you maybe only the name Herbert, and V. Herbert V. Once, I was very, very anxious, because he was correct. I went to the our office-- post office. I looked at the telephone book. And there-- and there were three names of Herbert with a V.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was thinking, to write to ask him, or not? He was [INAUDIBLE], of course. [INAUDIBLE] But then I think maybe it will be not good. Maybe he's married . Maybe this. He will-- so I didn't write. But this boss at this room was very correct, very good even.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure that-- ah, even once happened-- I never forget it-- morning by-- by marching he said to me, I follow you. In German he said to me, [NON-ENGLISH]. I didn't understand what's then. Probably, I was his type. Because at this, in Graudenz, the food was a little better. And as a boss, he had also. And maybe he was excited for somethings. I never [INAUDIBLE]-- I never forgot it. [NON-ENGLISH], I follow you. And then he had seen that very often the Hendrik when he used the toilet and when we went to the bath, instead-- and then instead to go up, he came to my bed. We joke. We joke. And this we talk, but very silent. Because the other prisoners, if they knew and see that there's something [INAUDIBLE], they should be probably next day joking, oh. But, frankly speaking, I felt that Hendrik had pleasure, because he left me some traces on my blanket, you know. So-- but he was so kind. He helped me this work, you know, the [INAUDIBLE]. So that I appreciated very much. And when I was sent in Graudenz to very good job, to magazine, with [? two ?]-- one German boy, I know the reason he was in prison. Because he'd steal the parcels for the soldiers. It was very dangerous. Maybe he was also hungry or stupid young. He was-- he has received even seven years in prison for the stealing, more worse than me. He was handsome boy. And he had some contact with the cook. I have seen the cook brought him some this. [INAUDIBLE] And I wanted to help Hendrik. But Heinrick was always only slimmer, slimmer. Maybe he is-- was ill. But then it was the winter time when you feel that it was so-- [NON-ENGLISH], chaos. In German-- In English, chaos. And we had to leave this. Then I have-- I had to pack. Because-- but no documents, only some others. Documents were left in Graudenz. The [? fall, ?] after the war, I had so big difficulties to find some documents. I wanted to have all documents. I wanted to see what I stated, what I had signed. I'd never seen it. And this, what I have received from Red Cross, this is one part. This mention it mother, father, where I'm born, et cetera. And there  I was punished for five years and nothing more. Or-- yeah.
